sections.heading: This Act is the Financial Advisers Act 2001.
sections.title: Definition of Financial Advisers Act 2001
sections.summary: This section establishes the Financial Advisers Act 2001 as the governing legislation for financial advisory services in Singapore. It defines key terms such as 'financial adviser', 'licensed financial adviser', 'financial advisory service', and 'approved exchange' to ensure clarity in regulatory compliance. The definitions are critical for understanding the scope of the Act and the responsibilities of financial advisers operating under its framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides foundational definitions that clarify the scope of financial data processing activities under the Act, particularly for cross-border data transfers involving financial advisers. It establishes terms like 'financial adviser' and 'financial advisory service' that directly impact data handling requirements.

requirements.text: This Act is the Financial Advisers Act 2001

sections.source_quote: This Act is the Financial Advisers Act 2001.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines key terms for financial compliance

sections.heading: This Act is the Financial Advisers Act 2001. (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: Pure commencement/assent date

sections.heading: This Act is the Financial Advisers Act 2001. (cont.)
sections.title: Financial adviser's licence requirements for service provision
sections.summary: A person must hold a financial adviser's licence to provide financial advisory services in Singapore. The licence must be granted by the Authority or the person qualifies as an exempt financial adviser. The definition of acting as a financial adviser includes activities likely to induce Singapore residents to use financial advisory services. The Authority may prescribe considerations for determining if activities constitute acting as a financial adviser.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the legal basis for financial advisers in Singapore, requiring licences for cross-border service provision and defining activities that constitute financial advisory services. It impacts DTIAs by setting the regulatory framework for who can provide services and how activities are assessed for cross-border applicability.

requirements.text: A person must not act as a financial adviser in Singapore in respect of any financial advisory service unless the person is authorised to do so by a financial adviser's licence or is an exempt financial adviser
A person is deemed to be acting as a financial adviser in Singapore if the person engages in activities likely to induce Singapore residents to use financial advisory services
The Authority may prescribe considerations for determining if activities constitute acting as a financial adviser

sections.source_quote: A person must not act as a financial adviser in Singapore in respect of any financial advisory service unless the person — (a) is authorised to do so in respect of that financial advisory service by a financial adviser's licence; or (b) is an exempt financial adviser.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial adviser's licence requirements for service provision

sections.heading: This Act is the Financial Advisers Act 2001. (cont.)
sections.title: Financial adviser's licence grant conditions
sections.summary: The Authority may grant a financial adviser's licence subject to conditions or restrictions, including limitations on the types of financial advisory services or investment products the licensed adviser can provide. The Authority can add, vary, or revoke these conditions at any time. Violations of conditions result in fines up to $100,000.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the conditions under which financial advisers can operate in Singapore, impacting DTIAs by requiring explicit approval for cross-border service provision and specifying how licence conditions affect data transfer and processing activities.

requirements.text: The Authority may grant any financial adviser's licence subject to conditions or restrictions regarding the type of financial advisory service
The Authority may add to, vary or revoke any condition or restriction of a financial adviser's licence
Any licensed financial adviser who contravenes a condition or restriction may be liable to a fine not exceeding $100,000

sections.source_quote: The Authority may grant any financial adviser's licence, subject to such conditions or restrictions as it thinks fit.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and penalties for financial data compliance

sections.heading: Any person who, in connection with an application for the grant or variation of a
sections.relevance_reason: regulator powers for data compliance

sections.heading: Any person who is aggrieved —
sections.title: Appeal against financial adviser licence refusal or revocation
sections.summary: Any person aggrieved by a financial adviser's licence refusal, variation, revocation, or suspension may appeal to the Minister within 30 days. The appeal must be in writing and addresses decisions made by the Authority regarding licence status. This section establishes a formal mechanism for challenging licensing decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a pathway for challenging licensing decisions that could impact data transfer operations if a financial adviser's licence is revoked or suspended.

requirements.text: Any person aggrieved by refusal, variation, revocation or suspension of financial adviser's licence may appeal to Minister within 30 days

sections.source_quote: 16. Any person who is aggrieved — by the refusal of the Authority to grant a financial adviser's licence to it, or to vary its licence; or by the revocation or suspension of its licence by the Authority, may, within 30 days of the refusal, revocation or suspension, appeal in writing to the Minister.
sections.relevance_reason: No data compliance elements
regulator powers for data compliance

sections.heading: [18/2015] (cont.)
sections.title: Auditor appointment and remuneration requirements for financial advisers
sections.summary: Licensed financial advisers in Singapore must appoint auditors to examine their books and remunerate them for duties imposed by the Authority. Auditors must submit reports to the Authority after completing audits. Contravention of remuneration obligations or audit reporting duties constitutes an offence. The Authority may appoint auditors if financial advisers fail to lodge reports or if the Authority receives reports under section 53(1).
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial advisers to maintain auditor oversight for book examination, with specific remuneration obligations and reporting duties to the Authority. This impacts cross-border data handling through auditor appointment and reporting requirements.

requirements.text: The licensed financial adviser must remunerate the auditor for duties imposed by the Authority
The auditor must submit a report to the Authority after completing examination and audit
The Authority may appoint auditors if financial advisers fail to lodge reports or if the Authority receives reports under section 53(1)

sections.source_quote: The licensed financial adviser must remunerate the auditor in respect of the discharge of such duty or duties as the Authority may impose on the auditor under subsection (2). Any auditor who contravenes subsection (1) or (2) shall be guilty of an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Auditor reporting to Authority for financial data compliance

sections.heading: [18/2015] (cont.)
sections.title: Prohibited businesses for licensed financial advisers
sections.summary: Licensed financial advisers in Singapore may only conduct authorized financial advisory services or businesses regulated by the Authority. They cannot engage in partnerships or other arrangements for prohibited businesses. The Authority may prescribe conditions for approved businesses. This section does not exempt financial advisers from other laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts cross-border business activities for financial advisers, requiring explicit authorization for international operations. Impacts data transfer by limiting permissible business activities and requiring regulatory approval for international engagements.

requirements.text: A licensed financial adviser must not carry on any business outside Singapore
A licensed financial adviser must not enter into partnerships or joint ventures for any business
A licensed financial adviser may only conduct authorized financial advisory services
A licensed financial adviser may only engage in businesses regulated by the Authority

sections.source_quote: A licensed financial adviser in Singapore must not carry on any business, whether in Singapore or elsewhere; or enter into any partnership, joint venture or any other arrangement with any person to carry on any business, whether in Singapore or elsewhere, except the following: (c) the business of providing any financial advisory service authorised by its financial adviser's licence; (d) any business, the carrying on of which is regulated or authorised by the Authority under any other written law or would be so regulated or authorised by the Authority if carried on in Singapore.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits cross-border financial activities for advisers

sections.heading: [18/2015] (cont.)
sections.title: Removal of financial advisers from office with due process
sections.summary: The Authority must not direct a licensed financial adviser to remove an individual from their office or employment without providing the adviser and individual with an opportunity to be heard, except if the individual is an undischarged bankrupt, subject to a prohibition order, or convicted of fraud/dishonesty punishable by imprisonment of 3 months or more. The adviser must notify affected directors within 30 days of a direction, and may appeal to the Minister within 30 days. The Minister's decision may modify the direction, and contravention incurs a fine up to $50,000.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires due process before removing individuals from financial adviser roles, impacting DTIA assessments for cross-border data transfers involving financial advisers where removal procedures affect data subjects.

requirements.text: The Authority must not direct removal without opportunity to be heard
Individual must be undischarged bankrupt
Individual must have active prohibition order
Individual must have been convicted of fraud/dishonesty punishable by 3+ months imprisonment
Adviser must notify affected director within 30 days
Adviser may appeal to Minister within 30 days
Minister may modify direction after appeal
Contravention incurs fine up to $50,000
No liability for reasonable care and good faith

sections.source_quote: The Authority must not direct a licensed financial adviser to remove an individual from his or her office or employment under subsection (2) without giving the licensed financial adviser and that individual an opportunity to be heard, except in any of the following circumstances: (a) the individual is an undischarged bankrupt, whether in Singapore or elsewhere; (b) a prohibition order against the individual has been made that remains in force; (c) the individual has been convicted, whether in Singapore or elsewhere, of an offence, committed before, on or after the date of commencement of section 7 of the Financial Institutions (Miscellaneous Amendments) Act 2024 — (i) involving fraud or dishonesty or the conviction for which involved a finding that the individual had acted fraudulently or dishonestly; and (ii) punishable with imprisonment for a term of 3 months or more.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and penalties for data compliance

sections.heading: [18/2015] (cont.)
sections.title: Control of licensed financial advisers
sections.summary: The Authority requires prior approval for any person to obtain effective control of a licensed financial adviser, with conditions on the applicant's fitness, the adviser's prudent conduct, and compliance with prescribed criteria. The Authority may impose conditions on share/voting power, and these conditions override company law. Effective control includes holding 20%+ shares, controlling 20%+ voting power, or having influence over policy decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs control mechanisms for financial advisers, impacting DTIA assessments for data transfers involving entities with foreign control structures.

requirements.text: Person must obtain Authority approval before gaining control
Applicant must be fit and proper
Adviser must continue prudent business conduct
Applicant must satisfy prescribed criteria
Authority may impose conditions on share/voting power
Conditions override Companies Act 1967
Effective control includes holding 20%+ shares
Effective control includes controlling 20%+ voting power
Effective control includes influence over policy decisions

sections.source_quote: A person must not obtain effective control of a licensed financial adviser that is a company, unless the person has obtained the prior approval of the Authority.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and conditions on control

sections.title: Electronic Gazette and Legislation Act 2025
sections.summary: This section refers to the Electronic Gazette and Legislation Act 2025, which provides a framework for electronic gazettes and legislation. It outlines the legislative process, including bill details, reading dates, and commencement dates for the act.

sections.heading: This Division applies to licensed financial advisers who provide any financial
sections.title: Disclosure of conflicts of interest in financial communications
sections.summary: Licensed financial advisers must disclose any interest in specified products or acquisition/disposal interests held by themselves or associated persons in circular communications. The disclosure must be in legible type, and must include financial benefits or advantages arising from specified products. The adviser must retain copies of such communications for 5 years. A defence exists if the adviser was unaware and could not reasonably be expected to know of the interest. The Authority may exempt certain persons or products from this section.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires disclosure of conflicts of interest in cross-border financial communications, impacting data transfer where advisers share product recommendations internationally.

requirements.text: Licensed financial adviser must include in circular communications a concise statement of any interest in specified products or acquisition/disposal interests held by themselves or associated persons
Disclosure must be in type not less legible than the rest of the communication
Adviser must retain copies of communications for 5 years
A defence exists if adviser was unaware and could not reasonably be expected to know of the interest
Interest includes financial benefits or advantages arising from specified products
Underwriting agreements constitute interests in acquisition/disposal of products
Persons are not connected unless acting jointly or under arrangement

sections.source_quote: Where a licensed financial adviser sends a circular or other similar written communication in which the licensed financial adviser makes a recommendation, whether expressly or by implication, with respect to any specified products, the licensed financial adviser must include in the circular or other communication, in type not less legible than that used in the remainder of the circular or other communication, a concise statement of the nature of any interest in, or any interest in the acquisition or disposal of, the specified products that the licensed financial adviser, or a person associated with or connected to the licensed financial adviser, has at the date on which the circular or other communication is sent.

sections.heading: [Repealed by Act 18 of 2022 wef 31/07/2024]
sections.title: Records to be kept by Authority
sections.summary: The Authority must maintain records of licensed financial advisers including their name, principal business address, types of financial advisory services, business name/style, officers removed under section 64, and any prescribed information. The Authority must also keep records of persons subject to prohibition orders under section 68. These records may be published or inspected by paying a fee, with certified extracts admissible as evidence in legal proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates record-keeping for licensed financial advisers and prohibited persons, which impacts data retention and access for DTIAs involving cross-border financial data transfers.

requirements.text: The Authority must keep records of licensed financial advisers including their name, principal business address, types of financial advisory services, business name/style, officers removed under section 64, and any prescribed information.
The Authority must keep records of persons subject to prohibition orders under section 68.
The Authority may publish the records or any part of them in an appropriate manner.
Any person may inspect and take certified extracts from the records upon payment of a prescribed fee.

sections.source_quote: 71. The Authority must keep in such form as it thinks fit records of licensed financial advisers setting out the following information of each of them: (a) its name; (b) the address of the principal place of business at which it carries on the business in respect of which the licence is held; (c) the type or types of financial advisory service and investment product to which its licence relates; (d) where the business is carried on under a name or style other than the name of the holder of the licence, the name or style under which the business is carried on; (e) officers removed by it from office or employment on the direction of the Authority under section 64; and (f) such other information as may be prescribed.

sections.heading: This Division does not apply to an inspection by a foreign regulatory authority of
sections.title: Exemptions for foreign regulatory inspections under consolidated supervision
sections.summary: This section exempts foreign regulatory inspections of Singapore financial advisers from the Division's restrictions if the foreign authority is an AML/CFT regulator exercising consolidated supervision over the adviser and the inspection is solely for that supervision purpose. The exemption applies to specified financial advisers as defined in section 20(1) of the Financial Advisers Act 2001.
sections.dtia_summary: Foreign regulatory inspections of Singapore financial advisers are exempt from this Division's restrictions when the foreign authority has consolidated supervision authority over the adviser and the inspection is solely for that purpose. This affects cross-border data transfer assessments involving financial advisers.

requirements.text: Foreign regulatory authority must be an AML/CFT authority as defined in section 17 of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022
Foreign regulatory authority must exercise consolidated supervision authority over specified financial adviser
Inspection must be solely for purposes of consolidated supervision

sections.source_quote: This Division does not apply to an inspection by a foreign regulatory authority of the books of a specified financial adviser in Singapore if — (a) the foreign regulatory authority is an AML/CFT authority as defined in section 17 of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 and exercises consolidated supervision authority as defined in that section over that specified financial adviser; and (b) the inspection is solely for the purposes of such consolidated supervision.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer exemption for AML/CFT

sections.heading: This Division does not apply to an inspection by a foreign regulatory authority of (cont.)
sections.title: Continuation of exemptions for foreign regulatory inspections
sections.summary: This section continues the exemption provisions for foreign regulatory inspections by specifying that the specified financial adviser, officer, auditor or person must comply with any conditions or restrictions imposed by the Authority under subsection (3) of section 82. The obligations continue after employment termination or appointment cessation.
sections.dtia_summary: Continuation of exemptions for foreign regulatory inspections that must comply with conditions imposed by the Monetary Authority of Singapore, affecting cross-border data transfer assessments.

sections.heading: In this Division —
sections.title: Definitions for investigation powers
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the Division, including 'computer', 'data', 'law enforcement agency', 'legal counsel', and 'officer' in relation to the Monetary Authority of Singapore. These definitions are critical for understanding the scope of investigative powers under the Division.
sections.dtia_summary: Definitions for investigation powers that affect cross-border data transfer assessments involving financial advisers.

requirements.text: Computer and data have meanings given by section 2(1) of the Computer Misuse Act 1993
Law enforcement agency means any authority or person charged with investigating offences
Legal counsel has meaning given by section 3(7) of the Evidence Act 1.893
Officer means person in relation to the investigated person

sections.source_quote: In this section, a reference to a requirement imposed by the Authority under this Division includes a reference to a requirement imposed by an investigator or authorised person under Subdivision (3) or (4).
sections.relevance_reason: Definitions critical for investigation powers

sections.heading: The provisions of this Subdivision apply where, pursuant to a requirement made
sections.title: Examination of examinee by investigator
sections.summary: This section requires that when an investigation under this Division occurs, a person (examinee) must appear before another person (investigator) for examination. The investigator may examine the examinee on oath or affirmation, and may administer an oath or affirmation to the examinee. The examinee must swear that the statements made during examination are true. The investigator may require the examinee to answer questions relevant to the investigation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes procedures for examinations during investigations, including oath-taking and question-asking, which may impact data transfer if the examinee's information is shared with regulators.

The investigator may administer an oath or affirmation to the examinee
The examinee must swear that statements made during examination are true
The investigator may require the examinee to answer questions relevant to the investigation

sections.source_quote: 91. The provisions of this Subdivision apply where, pursuant to a requirement made under section 90 for the purposes of an investigation under this Division, a person (called in this Subdivision the examinee) appears before another person (called in this Subdivision the investigator) for examination.

sections.title: Compliance with conditions on record copies

sections.heading: Where a person fails to comply with a requirement imposed by the Authority
sections.title: Failure to produce books or data by regulated persons
sections.summary: When a person fails to comply with the Authority's requirement under section 97 to produce books, provide information, or grant access to books/data, the Authority may require the person to state the location of such books or data, the last person with possession of such books/data, and the person who possesses the information. This applies to individuals and entities subject to the Authority's oversight under the Financial Advisers Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for locating data when compliance with data production requests fails, relevant for DTIAs involving data access and retention across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: State the place where books or data may be found
State the person who last had possession of books or data and their location

sections.heading: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires —
sections.title: Definitions for international assistance provisions
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the Part on assistance to foreign regulatory authorities, including 'foreign country', 'regulatory authority', 'investigation', and 'supervision'. These definitions are critical for understanding the scope of international data transfers and regulatory cooperation under the Financial Advisers Act.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify the scope of international data transfers and regulatory cooperation, relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border data sharing.

requirements.text: In this Part, 'enforce' means enforce through criminal, civil or administrative proceedings
'Foreign country' means a country or territory other than Singapore
'Investigation' means an investigation to determine if a specified person has contravened a law or regulatory requirement
'Material' includes any information, book, document or other record in any form
'Regulatory authority' means an authority of the foreign country exercising functions corresponding to Singapore's regulatory functions
'Relevant day' refers to specific dates for different financial advisory services
'Supervision' means taking action for the supervision of financial advisory services

sections.source_quote: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires — "enforce" means enforce through criminal, civil or administrative proceedings

This exclusion is relevant for understanding the scope of international data transfers and regulatory cooperation under the Financial Advisers Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section excludes specific assistance requests from the international data transfer framework, relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border data sharing.

sections.heading: In deciding whether to grant a request for assistance mentioned in section 108
sections.title: Additional factors for granting international assistance requests
sections.summary: When deciding whether to grant a request for assistance from a foreign regulatory authority, the Authority may consider whether the act would constitute an offence under the Act if it occurred in Singapore, whether the regulatory authority has given undertakings for future assistance, and whether the regulatory authority will contribute to costs. This applies to individuals and entities subject to the Authority's oversight under the Financial Advisers Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines additional factors for international data transfers, including legal equivalence and cost contributions, relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border data sharing.

requirements.text: Consider whether the act would constitute an offence under the Act if it occurred in Singapore
Consider whether the regulatory authority has given or is willing to give an undertaking for future assistance
Consider whether the regulatory authority will contribute to costs of providing assistance

sections.source_quote: In deciding whether to grant a request for assistance mentioned in section 108 from a regulatory authority of a foreign country, the Authority may also have regard to the following
sections.relevance_reason: regulatory oversight for cross-border data requests

sections.heading: Any person who —
sections.title: Refusal to comply with regulatory orders
sections.summary: This section defines offences for persons who refuse to comply with regulatory orders under section 108(1)(b), (c) or (d), provide false or misleading material in purported compliance with orders under section 108(1)(b) or (c), or make false statements under section 108(1)(d). Offenders face fines up to $10,000 or imprisonment for up to two years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes consequences for non-compliance with regulatory orders, which may impact data transfer compliance if regulatory orders relate to data handling across borders.

requirements.text: Refusal to comply with regulatory orders under section 108(1)(b), (c) or (d) without reasonable excuse constitutes an offence
Providing false or misleading material in purported compliance with orders under section 108(1)(b) or (c) constitutes an offence
Making false statements under section 108(1)(d) constitutes an offence

sections.source_quote: Any person who — (a) without reasonable excuse, refuses or fails to comply with an order under section 108(1)(b), (c) or (d); (b) in purported compliance with an order under section 108(1)(b) or (c), provides to the Authority or transmits to the regulatory authority any material or copy thereof known to the person to be false or misleading in a material particular; or (c) in purported compliance with an order made under section 108(1)(d), makes a statement to the Authority that is false or misleading in a material particular, shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $10,000 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 2 years or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: regulatory enforcement for data compliance

sections.heading: Where a person does an act partly in and partly outside Singapore which, if done
sections.title: Cross-border acts treated as Singapore offences
sections.summary: This section states that acts performed partly in Singapore and partly outside Singapore that would constitute an offence if done wholly in Singapore are treated as offences committed wholly in Singapore. This applies to regulatory compliance and data handling activities across borders.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that cross-border data handling activities are treated as Singapore offences if they would be offences under Singapore law if conducted entirely within Singapore, impacting data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: Acts partly in and partly outside Singapore that would constitute an offence if done wholly in Singapore are treated as offences committed wholly in Singapore

sections.source_quote: Where a person does an act partly in and partly outside Singapore which, if done wholly in Singapore, would constitute an offence under this Act, that person shall be guilty of that offence as if the act were carried out by that person wholly in Singapore, and may be dealt with as if the offence were committed wholly in Singapore.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data offences treated as Singapore offences

sections.heading: Where this Act provides for a person to be given an opportunity to be heard by
sections.title: Opportunity to be heard by Authority: prescribed procedures
sections.summary: This section allows the Authority to specify how persons must be given an opportunity to be heard when the Act requires such a hearing. The Authority may prescribe the manner of providing this opportunity, ensuring procedural fairness for affected individuals or entities under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section does not directly impact data transfer impact assessments as it concerns procedural mechanisms for hearings rather than data flows or cross-border processing.

requirements.text: The Authority may prescribe the manner in which a person is to be given an opportunity to be heard

sections.source_quote: Where this Act provides for a person to be given an opportunity to be heard by the Authority, the Authority may prescribe the manner in which the person is to be given an opportunity to be heard.
sections.relevance_reason: Authority procedures for hearings

sections.heading: Where this Act provides for a person to be given an opportunity to be heard by (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: regulator powers for data compliance

sections.heading: Where this Act provides for a person to be given an opportunity to be heard by (cont.)
sections.title: Electronic record authentication and admissibility standards
sections.summary: This section establishes requirements for electronic records to be admissible in evidence. It specifies that electronic records authenticated per subsection (7) or under the Evidence Act 1893 are valid. It also states that electronic records or copies thereof are not inadmissible in evidence simply because they lack paper counterparts. The section presumes accuracy of electronic records unless proven otherwise, and allows the Authority to make regulations for electronic service procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses electronic record authentication standards relevant to cross-border data transfers by defining acceptable authentication methods and ensuring electronic records are admissible in evidence without requiring paper copies.

requirements.text: Electronic records authenticated per subsection (7) or under the Evidence Act 1893 are valid evidence
Electronic records or copies thereof are not inadmissible in evidence solely due to lack of paper counterparts
Electronic records are presumed accurate unless proven otherwise
Authority may make regulations for electronic service procedures including handling service breakdowns

sections.source_quote: Singapore Statutes Online: Current version as at 09 Jul 2026
sections.relevance_reason: Electronic record admissibility standards for evidence

sections.heading: Where this Act provides for a person to be given an opportunity to be heard by (cont.)
sections.title: Regulatory exceptions for financial advisory services
sections.summary: This section defines exceptions where financial advisory services are considered incidental to other business activities. It includes legal professionals, accountants, trust companies, and newspaper proprietors who provide financial advice as part of their primary business. The section specifies conditions under which such advice is considered incidental to the core business activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section identifies entities that may provide financial advice without requiring full financial adviser licensing, relevant for data transfer assessments involving specialized services.

requirements.text: Legal professionals providing financial advice as part of their primary practice
Accountants providing financial advice as part of their primary accounting practice
Trust companies providing financial advice as part of their licensed activities
Newspaper proprietors providing financial advice as part of their newspaper business

sections.source_quote: Singapore Statutes Online: Current version as at 09 Jul 2026
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory exceptions for financial services

sections.heading: Any —
sections.title: Definition of Financial Advisers Act 2001
sections.summary: This section establishes the Financial Advisers Act 2001 as the governing legislation for financial advisory services in Singapore. It defines key terms such as 'financial adviser', 'licensed financial adviser', 'financial advisory service', and 'approved exchange' to ensure clarity in regulatory compliance. The definitions are critical for understanding the scope of the Act and the responsibilities of financial advisers operating under its framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides foundational definitions that clarify the scope of financial data processing activities under the Act, particularly for cross-border data transfers involving financial advisers. It establishes terms like 'financial adviser' and 'financial advisory service' that directly impact data handling requirements.

requirements.text: This Act is the Financial Advisers Act 2001

sections.source_quote: This Act is the Financial Advisers Act 2001.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines key terms for financial compliance

sections.heading: Any company licensed under the Trust Companies Act 2005 whose carrying on of the business of
sections.title: Research reports on investment products
sections.summary: This section mandates that financial advisers must issue or promulgate research analyses or reports in any format concerning investment products. These reports are considered advisory services and must be conducted by licensed professionals to ensure compliance with regulatory standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires research reports on investment products to be issued by financial advisers; relevant for DTIA when assessing if research reports are shared across borders.

requirements.text: Financial advisers must issue or promulgate research analyses or reports concerning investment products

sections.source_quote: 2. Advising others by issuing or promulgating research analyses or research reports, whether in electronic, print or other form, concerning any investment product.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for financial services

sections.heading: Any person who provides credit rating services, where any analysis or report issued or promulgated
sections.title: Credit rating reports without investment recommendations
sections.summary: This section specifies that credit rating services must issue reports that are solely incidental to their business activities and do not contain specific recommendations for acquiring, disposing of, subscribing for, or underwriting capital market products. These reports must not guide clients on investment decisions to avoid regulatory conflicts with market activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires credit rating reports to be non-recommendatory to prevent misuse in investment decisions; relevant for DTIA when assessing if credit ratings are used in investment contexts.

requirements.text: Credit rating reports must be issued solely as incidental to the provider's business of credit rating services
Credit rating reports must not contain specific recommendations for acquiring, disposing of, subscribing for, or underwriting capital market products

sections.source_quote: 5. Any person who provides credit rating services, where any analysis or report issued or promulgated by that person — (a) is issued or promulgated solely as incidental to the conduct of that person's business of providing credit rating services; and (b) does not contain any specific recommendation with respect to the acquiring of, disposing of, subscribing for, or underwriting of, any capital markets products.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data restrictions on credit ratings
